coredata.dc:title: Mechanism study on NO removal over the CQDs@MIL-100 (Fe) composite photocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Xianwei
Hu, Haoxuan
Qin, Junqi
Cao, Xi
Zhao, Ran
Wang, Dong
coredata.dc:description:
Novel composite photocatalysts MIL-100 (Fe) modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared via a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The photocatalytic performances were indagated through the gas phase NO removal experiment under visible-light illumination. Compared with the pure MIL-100 (Fe), the CQDs@MIL-100 (Fe) material showed excellent photocatalytic performance, with a maximum of 70% NO removal efficiency. The composite photocatalysts were systematically characterized with SEM, XRD, BET, DRS, PL, and FTIR. The results showed that CQDs were successfully coupled with the MIL-100 (Fe) via the C-O bond. The introduction of CQDs enhanced the utilization of sunlight and photoexcited-charge utilization efficiency, which in turn promoted the conversion of NO to NO
3
−
.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Denitration
MIL-100 (Fe)
Photocatalysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (No. 2016CFA076), the Hubei Provincial Department of Education Scientific Research Project, China (Grant D20181702), and the Innovation Team for Air Pollutant Generation and Control in the Textile Industry, China (Grant 201401003). The author would like to thank the Wuhan Advanced Fiber Engineering Technology Research Center for providing partial support for material handling.

This work was supported by the Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (No. 2016CFA076 ), the Hubei Provincial Department of Education Scientific Research Project, China (Grant D20181702 ), and the Innovation Team for Air Pollutant Generation and Control in the Textile Industry, China (Grant 201401003 ). The author would like to thank the Wuhan Advanced Fiber Engineering Technology Research Center for providing partial support for material handling.

C / M - x
(x
= 5
, 10, 15), (b) fresh (1) and used
C / M - 10
(2).

N

adsorption isotherms of (a) MIL-100 (Fe) and (b) C/M-10.

Photocatalytic removal of NO over C/M-10 with different scavengers.
The mechanism of NO removal on CQDs@MIL-100 (Fe) catalyst beneath visible-light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Mechanism study on NO removal over the CQDs@MIL-100 (Fe) composite photocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials & Application, Hubei International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Intelligent Textile Materials & Application, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials & Application, Hubei International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Intelligent Textile Materials & Application, Wuhan Textile University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials & Application, Hubei International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Intelligent Textile Materials & Application, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Novel composite photocatalysts MIL-100 (Fe) modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared via a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The photocatalytic performances were indagated through the gas phase NO removal experiment under visible-light illumination. Compared with the pure MIL-100 (Fe), the CQDs@MIL-100 (Fe) material showed excellent photocatalytic performance, with a maximum of 70% NO removal efficiency. The composite photocatalysts were systematically characterized with SEM, XRD, BET, DRS, PL, and FTIR. The results showed that CQDs were successfully coupled with the MIL-100 (Fe) via the C-O bond. The introduction of CQDs enhanced the utilization of sunlight and photoexcited-charge utilization efficiency, which in turn promoted the conversion of NO to NO
3
−
.

ce:section.ce:para: Excessive release of nitrogen oxides (NOx) causes environmental pollution such as photochemical smog, acid rain, haze, and holes in the ozone layer, and has attracted increasing attention in recent years (Ma et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017; Peng et al., 2017). Although flue gas denitration techniques, such as selective catalytic reduction (SCR) and selective non-catalytic reduction (SNCR), have been widely used (Li, 2017; Shang et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2010), these methods have the disadvantages of high operating costs and narrow temperature windows, and ammonia leaks. Recently, photocatalysis has attracted high attention due to its low cost, excellent performance, and environmental friendliness in removing nitrogen oxides (Klauson et al., 2016; Chong et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2015; Neves et al., 2017). It has obvious advantages compared with the reduction techniques. NO can be photocatalytically oxidized by sunlight without additional heating or adding oxidants. The photocatalysis mechanism for enhanced NO removal activity falls into the following categories: (1) increasing the surface vacancies and active sites. For example, Shi et al. (2019) prepared ultrathin structures of BiOBr/BiOI for photocatalytic removal of NO. (2) Construction of special hierarchical structures. For example, Ai et al. (2009) synthesized a new kind of BiOBr nanoplatelet microspheres photocatalyst. (3) Construction of heterojunctions. For example, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) modified by monodisperse bismuth nanoparticles were prepared by Jiang et al. (2017). However, many of those photocatalysts are still restricted to UV light excitations, resulting in the low usage of sunlight. Therefore, the exploitation of high-efficiency photocatalysis driven by visible light has become a research hotspot in the realm of photocatalytic denitrification.

The metal–organic framework (MOFs) is composed of a porous three-dimensional structure formed by transition metal cations and organic linking groups. Due to its variable structure, high porosity, and giant specific surface area, the application of gas storage, separation, and catalysis in the chemical industry is becoming more and more intense (He et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018; Alvaro et al., 2007; Shen et al., 2015; Fu et al., 2012; Kandiah et al., 2010). MIL-100 (Fe) is a typical MOFs photocatalyst. It has excellent chemical and water stability and can be used in photocatalytic denitration with improved utilization of light energy (Liang et al., 2015a; Xu et al., 2017; Liang et al., 2015b). However, the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in MIL-100 (Fe) limits its photocatalytic performance in the photocatalytic degrading of organic pollutants. To solve this problem, MIL-100 (Fe) was usually modified by metal doping, including Pd (Xu et al., 2018), Co [35], and Pt (Horiuchi et al., 2012), or by coupling photocatalyst, such as TiO 2 (Liu et al., 2017) and RGO (Liang et al., 2015c). However, a study on modifying MIL-100 (Fe) with carbon materials has rarely been reported.

Carbon quantum dot (CQDs) is currently one of the most popular new carbon nanomaterials due to its extremely small size, excellent optical properties, and good chemical stability. Moreover, CQDs can efficiently separate electron–hole pairs as an electron reservoir (Chen et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2013a; Ma et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2014b; Zhang et al., 2011). In this work, CQDs are used as photosensitizers to modify MIL-100(Fe). No precious metals or complex heterostructures are required. A new type of composite material was prepared by the strong chemical coupling between CQDs and MIL-100 (Fe), and the experimentation was executed in the photocatalytic removal of NO. The method to couple CQDs and MIL-100 (Fe) is simple and novel, which can be generalized for the synthesis of different quantum dots modified MOFs photocatalysts.

Two graphite carbon rods (99.99%, Alfa Aesar) with a spacing of 7.5 cm were placed into 500 ml of deionized water. A direct current (DC) power supply was connected to the graphite rods as the anode and the cathode to supply a voltage of 60 V. The water was stirred using a magnetic stirrer at 400 rpm. After 168 h, the anode was completely oxidized to form a dark yellow solution. The insoluble precipitate was removed by centrifugation. Finally, a water-soluble carbon quantum dots solution was obtained.

Analytically pure iron powder, ethanol, trimesic acid, HNO3, and HF were supplied by Shanghai Sinopharm Group Co. Ltd.) and were used without further purification (Liang et al., 2015b). Iron powder of 0.558 g and trimesic acid of 1.408 g were added to 49.86 ml deionized water, followed by adding HNO3 (0.266 ml) and HF (0.348 ml). The mixture was mechanically stirred for 5 min at room temperature before quickly placed into a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave, and then reacted at 150 °C for 24 h. After completing the reaction, the mixture was centrifuged, rinsed with ethanol and water, and then dried at 100 °C for 12 h. Light yellow colored MIL-100 (Fe) powder was yielded.

The synthetic process of CQDs@MIL-100 (Fe) is similar to that of MIL-100 (Fe), besides that CQDs aqueous solution is used in place of deionized water. The proportion of CQDs are changed by doping ratio of 5%, 10%, 15% with the same quality of MIL-100 (Fe) photocatalyst, which is recorded as C/M-5, C/M-10 and C/M-15 respectively.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the iron powder was acquired using a Cu K α radiation on a Bruker D8 Advanced X-ray diffractometer. The scanning rate was 10°/min with a 2 θ range of 10°-90°. The morphological characteristics of the samples were analyzed using a Philips Phenom Pro scanning electron microscope (SEM). Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms were recorded with a TriStar II3020 surface area analyzer (Micromeritics Instruments Corporation). An UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U4100) with a standard integrating sphere was used to measure diffuse reflectance UV–Vis spectrum over a detection range of 200–800 nm. Barium sulfate was employed as a reference standard. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer (Gangdong F-280) with a scanning rate of 1200 nm/min, and the slit width of 5 nm. FTIR spectra were measured using a VERTEX 70 Bruker spectrometer.

Scheme 1 shows a schematic diagram of the photocatalytic experiment setup. The homemade quartz reactor (inner radius: 60 mm, length: 10 mm) was filled with 0.5 g photocatalyst. It was positioned 5 cm underneath a xenon lamp ( λ > 420 nm, 500 W, PLS-SXE300; Beijing Perfect light). The simulated flue gaseous sample was composed of 8 vol%
O
2
in
N
2
and was doped with 500 ppm NO. The mass flow controller (MFC) was used to deliver the gas flow at 500 ml/min. The flue gas was uniformly mixed in the photocatalytic reactor. The NO concentration was monitored in real-time with a dedicated NO gas analyzer (KM950, Kane, UK). The experimental temperature is room temperature. The removal efficiency of NO was determined using Eq. (1):
(1)
η
NO
=
( 1 − C /
C
0
)
× 100 %

η
NO
- NO removal efficiency, %; C - NO outlet concentration, ppm;
C
0
- NO import concentration, ppm.

As demonstrated in Fig. 1a, the diffraction peaks at 2 θ of 27.7° (9321), 24° (6618), 20.1° (4814), 18.2° (7911), 14.2° (088), 11° (428) and 5.3° (333) were observed for MIL-100 (Fe), which is in good agreement with the previous study (Zhang et al., 2013b). Additionally, since no other diffraction peaks were observed, the sample was free from impurities. The similarity in the diffraction patterns between MIL-100 (Fe) and CQDs@MIL-100 (Fe) suggests that there are no major alterations in the skeleton structure of MIL-100 (Fe) after CQDs modification. Owing to the low content of carbon quantum dots and poor crystallinity, the diffraction peaks from CQDs were not found in the XRD patterns (Yu et al., 2014). It can also be observed that there were no significant differences in the diffraction peak position and intensity before and after the reaction, shown in Fig. 1b. This indicates that C/M-10 is stable which has no change in crystal structure during photocatalytic process.

From the SEM images in Fig. 2a, the MIL-100 (Fe) particles have a representative regular cubic octahedral structure with uniform crystal phase and smooth surface as well as sharp edges, which is consistent with the literature [4,25,41]. It can be seen from Fig. 2b that the introduction of CQDs has a significant impact on the morphology and structure of MIL-100 (Fe). The surface is rough and cracked, which are attributed to the dense CQDs layer on the surface. From both XRD and microstructure of the composite photocatalyst, it is suggested that CQDs have no measurable impact on the morphology of MIL-100 (Fe).

The BET adsorption isotherm in Fig. 3a exhibited typical microporous adsorption by MIL-100 (Fe). The specific BET surface area was 1571.31 m2/g. The adsorption isotherm of the C/M-10 composite photocatalyst is a representative type I adsorption isotherm, as shown in Fig. 3b, with the BET surface area of 1365 m2/g. Hence, the introduction of CQDs leads to a slightly decreased surface area, which did not affect the photocatalytic performance.

As shown in Fig. 4a, both MIL-100 (Fe) and C/M-10 specimens have high light absorption peaks in the ultraviolet region. After loading CQDs, the absorption edge of the composites in the visible light region has been enhanced from 624 nm to 700 nm. It can be concluded that the C/M-10 has a better visible light absorption ability. The photoabsorption range of MIL-100 (Fe) is broadened by coupling to CQDs due to the utilization of the luminescence from the CQDs, thus enhancing the photocatalytic performance [7,32]. This is attributed to that the CQDs can be partially excited by visible and near-infrared light. The low energy visible light can be converted to higher energy UV light by CQDs’ upconversion effect (Baker and Baker, 2010; Shen et al., 2012). Fig. 4b illustrates the Tauc plots of the photocatalysts which were calculated from the DRS data. By employing the Kubelka–Munk analysis, it can be concluded that the bandgap energy of C/M-10 (2.25 eV) decreases compared to that of the MIL-100 (Fe) sample (2.50 eV). Since the electronegativity of the semiconductor (X) and free electron energy (E
e
) of MIL-100 (Fe) are 5.10 eV and 4.5 eV respectively, the conduction (E
CB
) and valence (E
V B
) bands of MIL-100 (Fe) can be calculated as 1.85 and −0.65 eV, based on the Eqs. (2) and (3) (Yang et al., 2017).
(2)
E
V B
= X −
E
e
+ 0 . 5
E
g
(3)
E
CB
=
E
V B
−
E
g

The PL spectra of the two samples showed similar curvatures but with significant differences in the intensities, shown in Fig. 5. Under the 360 nm excitation light source, it was found that the C/M-10 sample produced a much lower PL intensity, indicating that the CQD helped to reduce the charge recombination, which could improve the photocatalysis efficiency.

The FTIR spectra of MIL-100 (Fe), fresh and used C/M-10 samples are displayed in Fig. 6. The MIL-100 (Fe) sample shows the vibration peaks at 1378, 1453, 759, and 711 cm−1 (Huang et al., 2017). Among them, the peaks at 1378 and 1453 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching modes of ν (CO) and ν (CO), respectively. The peaks at 759 and 711 cm−1 can be assigned to the out-of-plane bending modes of phenyl ν (CH). The peak at 628 cm−1 was assigned to the ν (FeO). After modification with the CQDs, the positions of the above four characteristic peaks remained unchanged, although the ν (CO) peak intensity was increased, indicating that the CQDs have been successfully coupled to the surface of MIL-100 (Fe) through the formation of CO bond. After photocatalytic NO removal, two sharp and narrow vibration bands were observed at 1278 and 1212 cm−1, which might be ascribed to the stretching of NO
3
−
(Wang et al., 2014c). This suggests that NO was oxidized to NO
3
−
during photocatalytic NO removal.

The results of photocatalytic removal of NO are presented in Fig. 7a. It demonstrates that with the increase of the CQD doping rate, the photocatalytic performance showed a volcano curvature that first increased and then decreased. The NO removal efficiency reaches a maximum of 70% when the CQD doping ratio is 10%, which is an increase of 29% in relative to that of pure MIL-100 (Fe). As shown in Fig. 7b and c, the composite catalyst is able to maintain good stability and excellent photocatalytic performance in a long-term photocatalytic environment. Fig. 7d also shows the NO conversion rate and NO2 selectivity of various samples. It can be concluded that the conversion rate of NO is in the range of 41 70% of different photocatalysts. The NO2 selectivity rate of C/M-10 (16.8%) is lower than that of MIL-100 (Fe) (53.2%), so the conversion rate of NO (70%) is higher than that of MIL-100 (Fe) (41%). The activity of C/M-10 is 1.7 times higher than that of MIL-100 (Fe), which are attributed to the maximum specific surface area of NO
3
−
adsorption and the effective charge separation ability of NO2 oxidation. The introduction of CQDs has crucial influences on the MIL-100 (Fe) in the following three aspects: (1) enhancing the visible light absorption; (2) the carrier migration was accelerated between interfaces; and (3) the charge recombination was effectively inhibited. As result, the CQD doping helps to generate more highly active free radicals. Therefore, the removal of NO by the composite catalysts was greatly improved. However, at very high CQD doping concentration, the light absorption will decrease due to the masking effects by the excessive CQDs, causing the decrease of the photocatalytic activity (Reddy et al., 2014).

The schematic diagram illustrating the CQDs@MIL-100 (Fe) photocatalytic NO removal is shown in Fig. 9. Under the visible light illumination, photoexcited electrons and holes were generated. Electrons transferred to the CQDs were reacted with
O
2
to generate superoxide radicals
O
2
−
, which participated in the NO oxidation reactions to produce NO
3
−
. The composite catalyst doped with CQDs effectively slowed down the charge recombination on the surface of the composite catalyst CQDs@MIL-100 (Fe). Hence, more hydroxyl radicals ( ⋅ OH) were generated from photo-generated holes to participate in the NO oxidation reaction, which resulted in NO2 and NO
3
−
. NO2 was further oxidized by the hydroxyl radicals to form NO
3
−
, which was the main product of oxidizing NO. This was confirmed by the FTIR characterization results.
(4)
CQDs / MIL − 100
( Fe )
+ h ν →
e
−
+
h
+
(5)
e
−
+
O
2
→
O
2
−
(6)

H

(7)

2

NO

NO
2

(9)

2

NO
3

+
H
+

(1) The composite photocatalysts were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal reaction. Compared with pure MIL-100 (Fe), the CQDs@MIL-100 (Fe) material shows excellent photocatalytic function, with a maximum NO removal efficiency of 70%.

(2) Based on the results of XRD, SEM, BET, DRS, PL, and FTIR, the photo-absorption wavelength generated by MIL-100(Fe) is broadened after CQDs doping. Meanwhile, CQDs are conducive to charge separation.

(3) Highly active radicals
O
2
−
and ⋅ OH were generated under the excitation of visible light. The NO bound on the surface of MIL-100(Fe) is oxidized to NO
3
−
by
O
2
−
, and NO is also oxidized to NO2 and NO
3
−
by ⋅ OH. NO
3
−
is the main product of NO oxidation.

Xianwei Fan: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Haoxuan Hu: Investigation, Validation. Junqi Qin: Software, Visualization, Validation. Xi Cao: Investigation, Validation. Ran Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Dong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (No. 2016CFA076), the Hubei Provincial Department of Education Scientific Research Project, China (Grant D20181702), and the Innovation Team for Air Pollutant Generation and Control in the Textile Industry, China (Grant 201401003). The author would like to thank the Wuhan Advanced Fiber Engineering Technology Research Center for providing partial support for material handling.
